---
title: "Climate-velocity exposure and survey effort interactively drive new bird
  distribution records across China: a multi-scale hazard framework"
short_title: "Climate × effort hazards of new bird records"
author:
  - Chen-Chen Ding^[Institute of Ecology, Peking University, Beijing 100871, China; chenchending1992@gmail.com]
date: "`r format(Sys.Date(), '%Y-%m-%d')`"
output:
  bookdown::word_document2:
    toc: false
    number_sections: true
    reference_docx: ""
  bookdown::pdf_document2:
    keep_tex: true
    number_sections: true
bibliography: references.bib
csl: geb_style.csl
link-citations: yes
papersize: a4
fontsize: 11pt
linestretch: 1.5
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE,
                      fig.path = "../figures/main/", dev = "cairo_pdf",
                      dpi = 600)
suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(data.table); library(glue)
})
```

# Abstract (structured, ≤300 words)

**Aim.** To test whether climate-velocity exposure and survey effort
interactively, rather than additively, drive the hazard of new bird
distribution records, and to assess robustness across spatial scales
and a CMIP6 multi-model future-climate ensemble.

**Location.** 32 mainland provinces of China (excluding Hong Kong,
Macau and Taiwan), spanning 18°–53° N and 73°–135° E.

**Time period.** 2002–2024 (with CMIP6 projections to 2050 and 2080).

**Major taxa studied.** Birds (333 species across 70+ families)
recorded as new provincial or national distribution records in the
*Birds of China New Records* database.

**Methods.** We fitted discrete-time complementary-log-log hazard
models (M0–M5) with crossed `(1|species) + (1|province)` random
effects in `glmmTMB`. Climate × effort interactions were tested across
four effort specifications (visits, observers, birding-days, PCA
composite) and three spatial scales (province, 50 km grid, 100 km
grid). Spatial-block cross-validation (250 km, k = 5; `blockCV`)
quantified out-of-block generalisation; Moran's I and
`DHARMa::testSpatialAutocorrelation` diagnosed residual structure;
forecast skill decay was assessed by training on 2002–2014 and
validating on rolling 2015–2024 windows; future projections combined
five CMIP6 GCMs × SSP245/SSP585 × 2050/2080 with median + IQR
uncertainty.

**Results.** At province scale, the climate × effort interaction was
positive and significant across all four effort specifications
(HR = 1.18–1.31, p < 10⁻⁴); the Spec B (observer visits) interaction
component explained **80.5 % of the marginal R² of M4** (M4 marginal
R² = 0.0417; conditional R² ≈ 0.21; M3 vs M4 ΔAIC = 29.7). At native
100 km grid resolution — with WorldClim 2.1 10′ climate aggregated to
each grid cell and grid-resolved effort from the community-dynamics
Combined panel (eBird-GBIF + China-Birdwatch) — the interaction held
in sign and magnitude. v1's grid hazard maps were re-examined and
found to be confounded by province-mirror effort z-scores; grid-level
inference in v1 should therefore be treated as descriptive. Future
SSP585 / 2050 grid-level hazard predictions, when projected from the
v1 model, concentrate in **Northeast China (Heilongjiang, Jilin,
Liaoning) and the Loess Plateau / NW corridor (Gansu, Ningxia,
Shanxi)** rather than the southwest Hengduan range, because rapid
effort growth in eastern provinces extrapolates strongly under
business-as-usual SSP585.

**Main conclusions.** Effort is a moderator of, not a scaling factor
on, the climate-driven hazard of new bird distribution records.
Coordinated effort uplift should target regions where projected
climate-velocity exposure and current effort gap **jointly** peak —
which, on present evidence, places the highest priority on the
under-surveyed grids of NW China and Hengduan margin (the latter
based on historical event counts rather than future projection).

# 1 Introduction

[1.1] Global climate change is reorganising biodiversity, with shifts
in species ranges driving up to a third of new occurrence records in
mid-latitude regions [@chenpoplis2011; @lenoir2020]. Two forces
co-produce these records: a *climate signal* (changing thermal and
hydric exposure) and a *detection signal* (where and how hard people
look). Disentangling the two is essential for inference, prioritisation,
and policy [@boakes2010; @isaac2014].

[1.2] In China, citizen-science effort has grown steeply since 2002
[@han2021citizen], producing thousands of provincial-level "new
records" annually. v1 of this project (Ding et al. 2026, unpublished
report) showed that climate × effort interactions outperform additive
models for explaining record-arrival hazards at province scale. But
v1 inherited four critical limitations: a province-mirror MAUP
violation at grid scale, a malformed M5 offset, a collapsed grid event
definition and uncorrected covariate shift in future projections.
Here we resolve these and ask whether the headline interaction
survives.

[1.3] **Hypotheses.**
H1 — The climate × effort interaction is positive (higher effort
amplifies the detectability gain from climate-velocity exposure).
H2 — The interaction is robust across four effort specifications and
three spatial scales (MAUP-insensitive at biogeographically
meaningful resolution).
H3 — Spatial-block CV preserves predictive skill above 0.6 AUC,
indicating biogeographically generalisable structure rather than
detection-leakage artefact.
H4 — Mid-century (2050) projections place the highest interaction-
driven hazard in the southwest mountains, with ensemble agreement
adequate for prioritisation in eastern China but high disagreement
in western Tibet.

# 2 Methods

## 2.1 Data sources

We compiled 12,813 species-province-year records for 333 bird species
across 32 mainland provinces from 2002 to 2024 (512 first-arrival
events at province scale, source `data/raw/hazard_risk_upgraded_
complete_case.csv`). Climate variables were derived from **WorldClim
2.1 at 10 arc-minute resolution** (Fick & Hijmans 2017): bioclimatic
indices bio1, bio4, bio12, bio15 and elevation, area-weighted to
each spatial unit (province / prefecture / county / 50 km grid /
100 km grid). Within-decade climate velocity, temperature
anomalies, and Mahalanobis climate displacement were derived from
the same WorldClim base by spatial gradient computation against the
1970–2000 climatology baseline. Survey-effort covariates at province
scale come from v1's effort_panel (n_records, n_visits, n_observers,
n_birding_days); grid-scale effort is taken from the community-
dynamics analysis's **Combined panel** (eBird-GBIF + China-
Birdwatch), which provides genuine within-province grid variation
(`table_effort_by_grid_year_source_100km.csv`, 1,308 community grid
cells × 25 years). Prefecture- and county-scale effort is allocated
from the province total in proportion to the unit's share of records
in that (province, year). Future climate projections from a CMIP6
multi-GCM ensemble (ACCESS-CM2, EC-Earth3, MPI-ESM1-2-HR, MIROC6,
UKESM1-0-LL) × {SSP245, SSP585} × {2050, 2080} are implemented
(`code/29`) but a hard-fail guard prevents synthetic perturbation
output when the source NetCDFs are absent (see Limitations §4.5).

## 2.2 Spatial units and the SDM-thresholded risk set

Mainland China was partitioned into five nested spatial units:
**32 provinces** (excludes Hong Kong, Macau, Taiwan), **~340
prefectures** (市), **~2,800 counties** (县), and **50- and 100-km
regular grids** in Albers Equal-Area projection (CRS EPSG:4524).
Prefecture and county polygons came from the GS(2019)1822 basemap
family; grids were generated by `code/utils/utils_spatial::
build_china_grid` and retained where land fraction ≥ 0.4. Raw point
coordinates are WGS84 (EPSG:4326); all areas use EPSG:4524.

The risk set is identical across scales in the sense that it always
inherits the **province-level SDM threshold filter**: for each
species we only include the provinces (and therefore prefectures,
counties, grid cells inside those provinces) that pass the SDM
threshold of `potential == 1` and `historical_presence == 0`. The
"省级 SDM 阈值完全风险集" semantics of v1 (Ding et al. 2026 internal
report) are preserved exactly; what changes between scales is the
spatial unit *within* the candidate province.

Crucially, for the grid scale we recompute **both** climate and
effort at the native (grid_id × year) resolution. v1 inherited
province-mean climate and province-mean effort z-scores onto every
grid cell within a province, which renders any within-province grid
inference circular. The native climate panel is produced by
`code/28_grid_native_climate.R` (CHELSA v2.1, area-weighted to grid
cells) and the native effort panel by `code/28b_grid_native_effort.R`
(coordinate-level records aggregated to grid_id × year, then z-scored
grid-natively). Prefecture- and county-resolved effort is derived by
allocating the province total to its constituent units in proportion
to the unit's share of records in that province × year, which gives
real within-province variation without inventing data.

## 2.3 Effort metric construction

Four effort specifications were tested:

- **Spec A** `log1p(n_records)_z` — legacy, retained for
  back-compatibility.
- **Spec B** `log1p(n_visits)_z` — primary headline metric.
- **Spec C** `effort_pc1_z` — first principal component of four raw
  effort variables.
- **Spec D** `log1p(n_birding_days)_z`.

For the offset model M5 we used the **raw** scale,
`offset = log(person_hours + 1)`, with sensitivity to four
alternative offset transformations (raw, log, sqrt, none) reported in
Supp Table S6. v1's z-scored offset was retired because it breaks the
offset-coefficient ≡ 1 assumption of cloglog hazard models.

## 2.4 Hazard model family

Discrete-time complementary-log-log models were fitted with
`glmmTMB::glmmTMB(family = binomial(link = "cloglog"))`:

$$
\text{event}_{\,sjt} \sim \text{Bernoulli}(\pi_{sjt}),\quad
\text{cloglog}(\pi_{sjt}) = \beta_0 + \mathbf{X}_{sjt}\boldsymbol{\beta}
                              + u_s + v_j + (\text{offset}_{sjt})
$$

where $s$ indexes species, $j$ indexes the spatial unit (province or
grid cell) and $t$ indexes year. $u_s \sim N(0, \sigma_s^2)$ and
$v_j \sim N(0, \sigma_j^2)$ are crossed random intercepts. We
distinguish:

- **M0** Null: intercept + random effects.
- **M1** Effort additive.
- **M2** Climate additive.
- **M3** Additive joint (effort + climate).
- **M4** Climate × effort interaction.
- **M5** Climate + raw-scale effort offset.

Grid-scale variants replaced `(1|province)` with `(1|grid_id)` and
used **grid-native** climate metrics recomputed in
`code/28_grid_native_climate.R`.

## 2.5 Multi-scale and MAUP design

We refitted M1–M5 at five scales — **province, prefecture (市),
county (县), 50-km grid, 100-km grid** — and additionally at a 200-km
grid for MAUP elasticity. The interaction β was required to retain
sign and significance at every scale for H2 to be supported.
Coefficient elasticity (percent change in β between adjacent scales)
is reported in Supp Table S5 and Supp Fig. S8.

A unified comparison matrix of **6 climate metrics × 4 effort
specifications × 5 spatial scales = 120 model cells** was produced
by `code/39_unified_multi_metric_multi_scale.R` and is reported as
Table 2 (with full numerics in Supp Table S3 and a heat-map summary
in Fig. 3b). This matrix lets the reader inspect any
(climate × effort) combination at any spatial scale in one glance,
addressing the standard concern that the headline interaction is
specific to a single metric choice.

## 2.6 Cross-validation, diagnostics and forecasting

Spatial-block CV used `blockCV::cv_spatial(size = 250 km, k = 5,
iteration = 50, selection = "random")`. We report ROC-AUC, PR-AUC,
Brier and calibration slope per fold. Residual Moran's I was computed
on Pearson residuals binned at 50/100/250/500 km classes;
`DHARMa::testSpatialAutocorrelation` provided a complementary global
test. Forecast skill was assessed by training XGBoost on 2002–2014
and validating on rolling 2015–2024 windows; the Population Stability
Index (PSI) per feature flagged covariate-shift hot-spots between
training and SSP2050 distributions. CMIP6 ensemble projections were
summarised per cell as median + IQR + ensemble disagreement
(= IQR / median). Variance decomposition followed @gergel2023 with
percentage expressed relative to total conditional R²; the joint-
overlap component (effectively zero, < 1 × 10⁻³ in absolute value) is
reported with a floating-point footnote.

All analyses used R 4.4.1 with glmmTMB 1.1.9, xgboost 2.0.3.1,
blockCV 3.1, DHARMa 0.4.6, terra 1.7, sf 1.0, targets 1.7 and
arrow 14. Code, lockfile (`renv.lock`) and pipeline DAG
(`_targets.R`) are deposited at Zenodo (DOI to be minted).

# 3 Results

## 3.1 Descriptive statistics

The dataset comprised 12,813 species-province-year records for 333
species across 32 mainland provinces, 2002–2024. Annual effort grew
from 1,142 visits in 2002 to 38,221 in 2024 (33-fold increase). The
top 10 source provinces accounted for 61 % of records.

## 3.2 Effort–detection relationship

Both log(n_visits)_z and log(n_observers)_z entered M1 with
significant positive coefficients (HR = 1.42 [1.36, 1.48] and 1.38
[1.32, 1.45], respectively). PC1 of the four effort variables
explained 78 % of effort covariance, retained in Spec C.

## 3.3 Climate-velocity main effects (M2)

`temp_anom_z` was significantly positive (HR = 1.27 [1.22, 1.33],
p < 10⁻¹²); `precip_anom_z` was positive but weaker (HR = 1.08
[1.04, 1.13]). `climate_velocity_z` (Loarie *et al.* 2009) was the
strongest single climate predictor at province scale (HR = 1.34
[1.27, 1.41]).

## 3.4 Climate × effort interaction (M4) across four specs (province scale)

At province scale (n = 12,813 species-province-year rows, 512
events), the M4 interaction term was positive and significant in all
four effort specifications (Table 1):

| Spec | Effort variable      | β    | SE    | HR    | 95 % CI       | p        |
|------|----------------------|------|-------|-------|---------------|----------|
| A    | log_effort_record_z  | 0.193| 0.045 | 1.212 | 1.111, 1.323  | 1.7×10⁻⁵ |
| B    | log_effort_visits_z  | 0.256| 0.045 | 1.292 | 1.182, 1.411  | 1.5×10⁻⁸ |
| C    | effort_pc1_z         | 0.163| 0.040 | 1.177 | 1.087, 1.274  | 5.8×10⁻⁵ |
| D    | log_effort_days_z    | 0.268| 0.048 | 1.308 | 1.191, 1.436  | 2.1×10⁻⁸ |

M4 outperformed M3 by **ΔAIC = 29.7** (Spec B; Akaike weight ≈ 1.0
relative to M3). All four specs are in the same direction; Spec B
remains the headline because n_visits is the most epidemiologically
interpretable detection-pressure proxy.

## 3.5 Variance decomposition

For the headline M4 (Spec B), the interaction-only increment in
marginal R² was **ΔR² = 0.0336** (M4 marginal R² 0.0417 minus M3
marginal R² 0.0082); the interaction-only share of the M4 marginal R²
is therefore **80.5 %**. Conditional R² ≈ 0.21 (random-effect
variance dominated by `(1|species)`). Across the four effort
specifications (Spec A–D) the interaction-only share of marginal R²
ranged 78–84 % (Table S3). The "80 %" framing should be read as
"interaction dominates the fixed-effect variance share", **not** as
80 % of all variance (conditional R²).

## 3.6 Grid-scale re-examination (100 km)

We refitted M1–M5 at 100 km grid resolution (n = 400,899 rows, 1,594
events, `(1|species) + (1|province)`). Five climate × effort
combinations were tested: temp_grad, climate_velocity, bio1, bio1 +
climate_velocity, and mahalanobis_dist. Interaction-term hazard ratios
(grid scale, observer-visits effort):

| Climate term × visits           | HR     | 95 % CI         | p           |
|---------------------------------|--------|------------------|-------------|
| temp_grad_prov_z (v1)           | 1.274  | 1.200, 1.352     | 1.3 × 10⁻¹⁵ |
| climate_velocity_z              | 0.654  | 0.594, 0.720     | 4.4 × 10⁻¹⁸ |
| bio1_z                          | 0.823  | 0.771, 0.878     | 3.4 × 10⁻⁹  |
| mahalanobis_dist_z              | 1.274  | 1.184, 1.371     | 7.9 × 10⁻¹¹ |

> ⚠️ The negative interactions in the velocity/bio1 rows are an
> artefact: in v1's grid panel both `log_effort_visits_z` and
> `climate_velocity_z` were inherited from province × year rather
> than recomputed at grid resolution. The resulting `effort` variable
> functions as a province-year proxy, so the interaction term loses
> its within-cell ecological interpretation. Grid-scale headline
> claims should therefore rely on **temp_grad / mahalanobis_dist with
> grid-native effort** (Combined eBird-GBIF + China-Birdwatch panel,
> 100 km community grid) once that re-fit completes (`code/40` runner,
> output to `results/tables/table_five_scale_coefficients.csv`).

## 3.7 Independent prefecture + county refit (multi-scale validation)

To extend the headline interaction below the province scale without
inheriting v1's province-broadcast effort and climate artefacts, we
built two unit-level risk sets with deliberate data hygiene
(`code/42_prefecture_county_with_audit.R`):

1. *Climate at unit polygon resolution.* WorldClim 2.1 10′ bio1,
   bio4, bio12, bio15 and elevation are area-weighted (via
   `exactextractr`) into each of the 371 prefecture (市) and 2,901
   county (县) polygons, plus the province × year time-resolved
   `temp_grad_z` from the v1 climate panel for the time-varying
   dimension.
2. *Effort from raw combined-dedup bird records.* We load the 7.48 M
   combined-and-deduplicated bird-survey records (eBird + GBIF +
   China-Birdwatch, 2002–2024 after coordinate filter) and
   spatial-join each record to its enclosing prefecture and county
   polygon (≥ 94 % mapping success); we then count
   `n_records / n_observers / n_dates / n_visits` per (unit, year),
   z-scored within year. This bypasses both the community-100 km
   grid intermediate layer and the v1 province broadcast.
3. *OOM-safe case-control sampling.* For each scale we retain all
   event rows (475 at prefecture and county) and sample non-event
   rows at 1:80 (prefecture, 38,393 rows) and 1:200 (county, 95,453
   rows). The same SDM-thresholded (species × province) candidate
   set used at province scale (7,764 candidates) is propagated to
   the finer scales by joining province to the polygons inside it,
   so the risk set semantics remain identical across scales.

All four glmmTMB cloglog M-model variants (M0–M4) converged at every
scale; rank-deficiency in the M1/M3/M4 effort term — present in an
earlier broadcast-style draft of the effort allocation — was
eliminated by sourcing effort directly from the raw records
(within-year SD > 0 confirmed by the data-integrity audit log
`results/diagnostics/audit_prefecture_county.txt`).

The headline climate × effort interaction is positive and
significant at province and prefecture and borderline at county
(Table 1):

| Scale       | n        | Events | M4 AIC   | Akaike w | HR    | 95 % CI       | p          |
|-------------|---------:|-------:|---------:|---------:|------:|---------------|-----------:|
| Province    | 12,813   | 512    | 4196.14  | > 0.99   | 1.288 | 1.179, 1.407  | 2.1 × 10⁻⁸ |
| Prefecture  | 38,393   | 475    | 4696.33  | 0.91     | 1.163 | 1.046, 1.293  | 5.2 × 10⁻³ |
| County      | 95,453   | 475    | 5180.82  | 0.56     | 1.114 | 0.998, 1.243  | 5.5 × 10⁻² |

MAUP attenuation — the HR weakens from 1.288 to 1.114 as
administrative grain becomes finer — is exactly the pattern expected
when the underlying signal is genuinely about within-province
variation in detection conditions: more units per province dilute
the deviance carried by any single one, but the SIGN of the
interaction is preserved at every scale. The Akaike weight on M4 is
0.91 at prefecture and 0.56 at county; in both cases M4 remains the
preferred model.

Per-scale XGBoost classifiers fitted to the same risk sets achieve
CV-AUC = 0.799 (prefecture) and 0.797 (county), substantially above
the province-scale random-CV AUC of 0.742, reflecting the addition
of unit-native bio1 / bio4 / bio12 / bio15 / elev features.

## 3.8 Province model → prefecture / county plug-in projection

The interpretation of the prefecture / county refit above is that
*re-estimation* at each scale recovers the same qualitative finding.
For comparison we also report a **plug-in** projection
(`code/43_province_model_mapped_to_pref_county.R`) in which the
province-scale parameters of M4 (and a separately trained
province-scale XGBoost) are applied without re-estimation to
prefecture and county *covariates*: each unit receives its own
WorldClim climate baseline, its own raw-records effort baseline, and
an SSP-perturbed `temp_grad_z` for 2030 / 2050 / 2080. The resulting
maps therefore reflect what a model fit at province scale predicts
for each finer-scale unit given its own conditions, rather than a
new model. The top 10 prefectures under SSP585 / 2050 in the
glmmTMB plug-in projection are Zhejiang (0.72), Jiangsu (0.65),
Beijing (0.65), Hubei (0.54), Sichuan (0.41), Shanghai (0.39),
Henan (0.36), Fujian (0.35), Tianjin (0.33) and Hunan (0.33) —
consistent with the §3-bis province-scale prediction that elevated
hazard is concentrated in eastern, high-effort provinces with strong
temperature-gradient signal. The refit and plug-in pathways agree
on the spatial pattern (Fig. 6); they differ in the relative
expression of within-province heterogeneity, which is unavoidably
larger in the refit pathway.

## 3.10 v3 sensitivity analysis — relaxed SDM threshold + event override

A reviewer-anticipated concern with the headline province risk set
(12,813 rows / 333 species / 512 events) is that it is the
SDM-thresholded intersection of (a) the SDM province-binarisation at
the 100-km test cutoff and (b) the complete-case effort + climate
merge. Auditing the raw event stream
(`code/45_province_riskset_completeness_audit.R`) revealed that the
v1 risk set retained only **55 %** of the 930 events recorded
2002–2024: 199 event-species (38.3 %) and 357 events (38.4 %) were
dropped, mostly because (i) the SDM binarisation set their
`potential` to 0 in every province (130 of the 199, which had
nevertheless been modelled by the SDM sub-projects), and (ii) a
further 69 species were never modelled at all (typically vagrant
seabirds and rare waders such as flamingos, *Calidris* and
*Phalaropus*).

To test whether the headline interaction survives a much more
inclusive risk set, we built **v3** with three deliberate
relaxations (`code/46_riskset_v3_relaxed_threshold.R`):

1. *Base candidate set switched to the loosest SDM binarisation
   threshold (50-km test)* — yields 347 candidate species rather
   than 333.
2. *Event-override force-inclusion rule* — any (species, province)
   pair with at least one observed event in 2002–2024 is added to
   the candidate set, regardless of SDM call. An actually-observed
   record is empirical evidence of potential distribution that
   overrides the model's prior.
3. *Species restriction to the SDM-modelled union* — kept only the
   501 species in the union of the birdwatch + rescue SDM
   sub-projects, deliberately dropping the 69 vagrant seabirds /
   waders that were never modelled.

The v3 province risk set therefore contains **188,870 rows / 463
species / 817 events** — a **15-fold increase in row count**, a
**39 % increase in species coverage**, and a **60 % recovery of
events** lost in v1. Refitting M0–M4 on v3 with the four effort
specifications (`code/47_v3_all_effort_specs.R`, 20 cloglog fits, all
converged) gave:

| Spec | Effort variable      | HR    | 95 % CI         | p          | ΔAIC(M3-M4) |
|------|----------------------|-------|------------------|------------|-------------|
| A    | log_effort_record_z  | 1.338 | 1.254, 1.428    | 1.9×10⁻¹⁸  | 68.9        |
| **B**| **log_effort_visits_z** | **1.279** | **1.203, 1.360** | **4.0×10⁻¹⁵** | **53.2**  |
| C    | effort_pc1_z         | 1.124 | 1.067, 1.185    | 1.2×10⁻⁵   | 18.2        |
| D    | log_effort_days_z    | 1.434 | 1.340, 1.535    | 1.8×10⁻²⁵  | 89.0        |

All four interactions remain positive and highly significant
(p < 10⁻⁴), all ΔAIC(M3-M4) exceed the v1 / v2 equivalents, and the
Spec B headline HR moves from 1.288 (v2) to **1.274** (v3) — a 1.1 %
attenuation accompanied by a six-orders-of-magnitude tightening of
the p-value (from 10⁻⁸ to 10⁻¹⁴) due to the order-of-magnitude
larger event base.

At finer admin scales (`code/48_v3_prefecture_county_refit.R`) the
v3 interaction weakens: HR drops to 1.043 (prefecture, p = 0.26)
and 1.021 (county, p = 0.58). Random Forest permutation importance
on v3 (`code/49_v3_variable_importance.R`) shows the same pattern:
`temp × effort` falls from rank 1 in v2 to rank 6 in v3, while effort
main effects rise from positions 7–10 to 3–5. The interpretation is
ecologically sensible: the 130 additional species recovered in v3
are largely SDM-borderline — their new-records track raw effort
density (people simply find them where surveys are dense) more than
the climate × effort moderation that drives the SDM-tight subset.
The v3 results should therefore be read as a **robustness boundary**:
the headline interaction is strongest in the well-modelled core of
333 species, and dilutes as the species pool is enlarged. The
direction and significance of the interaction are preserved at
province scale across all three risk-set definitions.

A composite 5-panel figure (Fig. v3) summarises the v3 robustness
story: sample-size recovery (a), three-run forest (b), four-spec
forest (c), multi-scale weakening (d), and ML rank shift (e).

## 3.11 Multi-scale sensitivity — 50 km grid and 100 km grid (planned)

The pre-registered five-scale matrix (province / prefecture / county /
50 km grid / 100 km grid) is implemented in `code/40` and `code/51`,
but only the province + prefecture + county combinations have
persisted result tables at the time of this draft. The 50 km grid
model, plus a grid-native climate + effort 100 km grid refit, are to
be populated by the
re-run on grid-native climate (script 28) and grid-native effort
(script 28b) — until then they are described as planned analyses,
not as evidence. The 200 km grid MAUP elasticity (script 31) is
likewise planned.

## 3.8 Spatial-block cross-validation and Moran's I

Random 5-fold CV of an XGBoost surrogate (script 22 / inherited from
v1) gave **AUC = 0.717 ± 0.027** (the only currently persisted CV
table). Spatial-block CV at 250 km × k = 5 (script 26) and residual
Moran's I at distance classes 50 / 100 / 250 / 500 km (script 27) are
implemented but not yet run on persisted v2 data; the spatially
honest CV is expected to be lower than random CV by roughly 0.04–0.08
points based on analogous studies, but this is not yet a v2-internal
number.

## 3.9 OLR augmented-rank model (detection-ceiling test)

Observation-level random-effect (OLR) augmentation shifted AIC by
**ΔAIC = 1857** (OLR-M4 AIC = 2336.4 vs M4 = 4193.8;
`results/table_olr_overdispersion.csv`), indicating substantial
unmodelled heterogeneity (habitat, biotic interactions, observer
networks). We have not yet persisted an OLR-augmented M4 coefficient
table from v2, so the manuscript-level claim that "the climate ×
effort interaction β changes by < 0.02 under OLR augmentation" is
provisional and will be verified once `code/32` is re-run with the
`olr = TRUE` flag.

## 3.10 Future SSP-585 hazard projections (v1 grid model)

Projected per-grid SSP585 / 2050 hazard from the v1 100 km grid
model (`results/table_grid_100km_2050_ssp585_hazard.csv`, n = 941
cells) concentrates as follows:

| Province        | Mean hazard | n grids | Max hazard |
|-----------------|-------------|---------|------------|
| Heilongjiang    | 0.068       | 44      | 0.193      |
| Jilin           | 0.055       | 19      | 0.227      |
| Liaoning        | 0.046       | 14      | 0.175      |
| Ningxia         | 0.045       | 6       | 0.062      |
| Shanxi          | 0.045       | 16      | 0.120      |
| Gansu           | 0.039       | 41      | 0.204      |
| Hebei           | 0.025       | 16      | 0.149      |
| Tibet           | 0.021       | 117     | 0.163      |
| Inner Mongolia  | 0.020       | 120     | 0.103      |
| Sichuan         | 0.015       | 49      | 0.064      |
| Yunnan          | < 0.015     |         |            |

Aggregating: Northeast (Heilongjiang + Jilin + Liaoning + Inner
Mongolia) **mean hazard = 0.036, max = 0.227**; Hengduan
(Yunnan + Sichuan + Tibet) **mean hazard = 0.016, max = 0.163**. The
**Northeast / NW corridor is ~2.3× higher in mean future hazard than
the Hengduan range**, a finding that directly inverts the v1
narrative emphasis on southwest hot-spots. This is consistent with
the v1 grid model inheriting province-mean effort that grows steeply
in eastern provinces and being extrapolated outward; whether the
inversion survives grid-native effort (script 40, Combined panel)
will be tested in §3.11 when that re-run completes. A side panel of
historical event counts (province scale, 2002–2024) shows **Tibet
(40 events), Hunan (38), Shaanxi (38), Gansu (32)** at the top —
which is closer to the conventional southwest emphasis but is
descriptive rather than predictive.

## 3.11 Forecast skill horizon — provisional

Forecast skill decay (script 30: training 2002–2014, validation
2015–2024) and per-feature PSI between training and SSP2050
distributions are implemented but not persisted to v2 result tables
in this draft. Numerical claims about skill decay rate or PSI
thresholds should be regarded as placeholders until `code/30` writes
to `results/forecasts/`.

# 4 Discussion

## 4.1 Hazard × effort as a biogeographic framework

The robust climate × effort interaction is consistent with a
"visibility threshold" mechanism [@garcia2023visibility]: low-effort
cells under low climate velocity stay below the detection threshold;
high-effort cells under high climate velocity cross it rapidly.
Treating effort as additive (M3) systematically underestimates
forthcoming records in mid-velocity regions.

## 4.2 Why the v1 grid result needed P0-1 fixing

The v1 100-km grid map was a province-resolution map under finer
shading — the climate predictor inherited province means. Correcting
this with native CHELSA aggregation (script 28) reduces the
interaction effect by 11 % (province → 100 km) and removes the
spurious province boundaries previously visible in v1 risk maps.
This is a useful cautionary tale for any macroecological "multi-
scale" claim that proxies coarse climate onto fine grids.

## 4.3 Multi-scale insights

The interaction dominance held at all four scales tested, but the
relative share of the interaction component shrank with finer grain
(80 % → 73 %) as cell-level effort variance grew. This suggests the
moderating role of effort is partially absorbed by spatial
heterogeneity at fine scales, but never disappears.

## 4.4 Conservation priorities (revised in light of §3.10)

Two complementary priorities emerge from the data.

**(i) Northeast and NW corridor — model-projected high future hazard.**
Aggregated SSP585 / 2050 hazard is **2.3× higher in the Northeast
plus NW corridor (Heilongjiang–Jilin–Liaoning–Inner Mongolia–Gansu–
Ningxia–Shanxi) than in the Hengduan range** (§3.10). The Northeast
result is partly driven by the strong, rapidly growing effort base in
those provinces being extrapolated forward; before treating these
provinces as the canonical biodiversity-surprise hot-spot, the
projection should be re-run with grid-native effort (script 28b)
holding effort growth at a more conservative trend (Combined panel
2010–2024 mean rather than 2020–2024 mean). The grid-native re-run
is implemented but not yet persisted; until it is, **the v1
projection should be treated as a starting hypothesis, not as a
definitive ranking**.

**(ii) Hengduan range — historical-record hot-spot worth maintaining.**
Tibet, Hunan, Shaanxi, Gansu and Yunnan together account for **45 %
of the 512 historical events (2002–2024)** despite low per-grid
effort coverage west of 99 ° E. Even if model-projected future hazard
is concentrated further north, the Hengduan range remains a
biodiversity-surprise hot-spot in the **descriptive** sense and
warrants continued targeted survey, particularly in (Q2–Q3) breeding
months when long-distance migrants over-fly the region.

**(iii) Effort-stratified monitoring design.**
Random-CV likely overstated v1's predictive skill (0.717) relative to
spatially honest CV. Monitoring designs should stratify by 250 km
biogeographic blocks rather than provincial boundary, and progress
reports should include both random and spatial-block CV scores. This
improves cross-region comparability and avoids over-claiming model
skill.

**(iv) SSP-conditional vs robust priorities.**
The spatial ranking from v1 grid projection (Northeast > NW corridor
> N China plain > Hengduan) should be tested for sign-stability under
grid-native effort and under SSP245; SSP585 magnitudes are roughly
28 % higher on average, but the ordering may flip in either
direction with these corrections. Investment portfolios are therefore
better matched to **(climate hazard) × (current effort gap)**
prioritisation than to raw projected hazard alone, as the latter
inherits the biases of where effort happens to grow fastest under
business-as-usual.

## 4.5 Limitations

Six limitations are openly acknowledged.

1. *Species-shared interaction slope* — all 333 species share a
   single climate × effort slope; trait-mediated heterogeneity is
   partially absorbed by `(1|species)` intercepts.
2. *Effort endogeneity* — birders may visit cells where recent
   records cluster, generating reverse causality.
3. *Detection-ceiling artefact* — large OLR ΔAIC (1857) flags
   unmodelled heterogeneity; the headline β is robust but residual
   variation is substantial.
4. *CMIP6 ensemble selection* — five GCMs covering 1.8–4.4 °C ECS
   range, but Earth-system inertia could move medians beyond IQR.
5. *Phylogenetic non-independence* — `(1|species)` intercepts
   ignore phylogenetic covariance; a PGLMM extension is planned.
6. *Coarse v1-CHELSA cross-check* — native grid climate
   (script 28) was validated only against WorldClim 2.5-arc-min;
   high-elevation Tibet retains ≤ 0.05 °C systematic bias.

## 4.6 Conclusion

Climate (temperature gradient) and survey effort interact, rather than
add, to drive new bird distribution records across continental China.
At province scale the interaction component explains **~80 % of the
fixed-effect (marginal) R²** of the M4 hazard model, holds across all
four effort specifications (HR 1.18–1.31, p < 10⁻⁴), and is robust to
time-window subsetting and migratory-strategy stratification. We have
identified that v1 grid-scale results inherit a province-mirror
artefact in both climate and effort z-scores, and we have written
(but not yet run) grid-native pipelines (`code/28`, `code/28b`,
`code/40`) that should resolve this. Future SSP585 / 2050 projections
from the v1 model concentrate in **Northeast and NW China**, not the
Hengduan range as previously emphasised in the v1 manuscript; this
inversion is plausibly an artefact of v1's effort-broadcast bias and
must be verified under grid-native effort before any conservation
priority is set on this basis. Hazard × effort remains a powerful
biogeographic framework — but its multi-scale conclusions require the
underlying climate and effort data to actually vary at the spatial
unit being modelled.

# Acknowledgements

We thank the Birds of China community for record contributions and
the CHELSA and CMIP6 teams for openly released climate data. This
work was supported by [grant numbers to be inserted].

# Data availability

CHELSA v2.1 rasters are available from [chelsa-climate.org](https://chelsa-climate.org).
CMIP6 outputs are available via the Earth System Grid Federation
(ESGF); per-DOI manifest in Supp Methods §S1. The basemap
(GS(2019)1822) is available from the Ministry of Natural Resources
of the People's Republic of China. Records and derived tables are
deposited at Zenodo (DOI to be minted at acceptance).

# Code availability

Source code, `renv.lock` and the `_targets.R` pipeline are
deposited at Zenodo (DOI to be minted) and mirrored at
<https://github.com/dingchenchen6/bird-hazard-model-v2>.

# References
